data.title: Mortgage rates rise but purchase application demand remains solid
data.description:
Mortgage rates rose this week but remained below 7%, while purchase application demand remained strong, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.83% as of April 17, higher than 6.62% the prior week but lower than 7.10% in the year-ago week.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.03%, compared to 5.82% a week ago and 6.39% a year ago.

"The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage ticked up but remains below the 7% threshold for the thirteenth consecutive week," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

"At this time last year, rates reached 7.1%, while purchase application demand was 13% lower than it is today, a clear sign that this year’s spring homebuying season is off to a stronger start," added Khater.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed names Anna Paulson to succeed Harker as president
data.description:
The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia named Anna Paulson as its next president and CEO, succeeding Patrick Harker, whose term ends June 30, 2025, the district reserve bank said on Thursday.

Paulson is currently executive vice president and director of research at the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago. She'll take office on July 1, 2025, the Philly Fed said.

She has 20 years of experience in policy analysis and research related to financial stability and monetary policy. As executive vice president and director of research at the Chicago Fed, Paulson advises the bank's president and CEO on monetary policy and attends Federal Open Market Committee meetings.

Following the rotation schedule of district Fed bank presidents, The Philly Fed president will next be a  voting member of the FOMC in 2026.

data.title: Fed's Barr urges banks to adopt AI to combat rising deepfake risks
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr on Thursday urged banks to adopt AI-powered tools, such as facial recognition, voice analysis and behavioral biometrics, to enhance customer identity verification and counter growing deepfake risks.

A deepfake is a fake piece of digital content created with AI to ultimately replicate a real person's identity.

Such technology "has the potential to supercharge identity fraud," Barr warned in remarks prepared for delivery at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

His solution: "we should take steps to lessen the impact of attacks by making successful breaches less likely, while making each attack more resource-intensive for the attacker."

The Fed's former top banking cop noted that lenders already have dual oversight of transactions, verifying both the sender's identity and the recipient's address. In addition to "employing advanced analytics to flag suspicious patterns that could indicate fraudulent activities," banks are already "engaging with other financial institutions to help define the threat and identify appropriate controls and mitigants," he added.

It's also worth exploring how regulators can use AI to "enhance our ability to monitor and detect patterns of fraudulent activity at regulated institutions in real time," he said. This could help provide early warnings to affected institutions and broader industry participants, as well as to protect our own systems."

Earlier this month, Bloomberg reported that hackers spied on the emails of around 100 banking regulators at the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency for more than a year, infiltrating messages comprising agency deliberations and sensitive details about the banks it oversees.

data.title: Unitedhealth group revises FY2025 EPS outlook to $26-$26.50 amid higher care activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Witty reported that the company observed "continued strong growth across our businesses," but also noted "an overall performance that was frankly unusual and unacceptable." The adjusted earnings per share outlook for the year has been revised to $26 to $26.50.
  • Higher-than-expected care activity in UnitedHealthcare's Medicare Advantage business during Q1 2025, particularly in physician and outpatient services, was a significant factor. This increase was limited to the Medicare Advantage segment and did not affect commercial or Medicaid businesses.
  • An influx of new Medicare patients at Optum Health, particularly from plans exiting markets and with lower-than-expected reimbursement levels, was highlighted as an issue impacting revenue. These challenges were compounded by CMS risk model changes.
  • Key initiatives include enhancing member engagement, improving the transition to the new CMS risk model, and updating Medicare Advantage plan designs for 2026.
  • CFO John Rex emphasized that "we now expect adjusted earnings of $26 to $26.50 per share," reflecting higher care activity and changes in patient profiles. Revenue guidance remains at $450 billion to $455 billion.
  • Operating earnings outlooks for UnitedHealthcare and Optum Health have been adjusted, with specific impacts attributed to care activity and patient mix changes.

Outlook

  • Full-year medical care ratio is expected to be 87.5%, reflecting increased care activity and patient mix changes.
  • Revenue outlook for Optum Health is set at $106 billion to $107 billion, with operating earnings projected between $6.2 billion to $6.4 billion.
  • UnitedHealthcare's operating earnings outlook has been revised to $16 billion to $16.5 billion.
  • Plans for 2026 Medicare Advantage include addressing the trends observed in 2025 and incorporating them into pricing and plan designs.

Financial Results

  • The company reported care activity in Q1 2025 at twice the expected rate, particularly in the Medicare Advantage business.
  • Optum Rx revenues grew by 14% to exceed $35 billion in the quarter, supported by customer retention and new wins.
  • Consolidated revenue remains consistent with previous guidance, at $450 billion to $455 billion for the full year.
  • Adjusted earnings per share guidance was lowered to $26 to $26.50 for FY2025, reflecting changes in care activity and patient profiles.

Q&A

  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research, inquired about the Medicare Advantage cost trend and the company’s assumptions. Tim Noel responded, explaining the doubling of care activity in Q1 2025 and the assumption that it will persist throughout the year and into 2026.
  • Josh Raskin, Nephron Research, questioned the connection between higher primary care visits and Optum Health's pressure. Dr. Amar Desai elaborated on the challenges from new Medicare patients and the CMS risk model transition.
  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo, asked about Medicare Advantage margins and the timeline for recovery. Tim Noel stated that margins are expected to remain within targeted ranges for 2025.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over unexpected increases in care activity and its implications for margins and future guidance. Questions were critical yet constructive, seeking clarity on management’s actions.
  • Management showed a combination of cautious optimism and determination to address the challenges, emphasizing the temporary nature of current issues and confidence in long-term strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was more defensive, reflecting the unexpected performance challenges in Q1 2025.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Adjusted EPS guidance has been revised down from the initial outlook provided in Q4 2024.
  • Significant shifts in care activity trends and patient profiles were identified in Q1 2025, contrasting with a more stable outlook in Q4 2024.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general performance to specific impacts of Medicare Advantage trends and Optum Health’s challenges.
  • Management’s tone in Q1 2025 was more focused on addressing performance shortfalls, compared to a more forward-looking and optimistic tone in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased care activity in Medicare Advantage beyond expectations poses a risk to profitability.
  • Challenges in transitioning to the new CMS risk model impacted revenue and operational efficiency.
  • Medicaid funding adequacy and Medicare Advantage pricing adjustments remain areas of focus.
  • Potential regulatory changes in pharmacy benefit management could impact Optum Rx operations.

Final Takeaway

UnitedHealth Group highlighted both significant challenges and actions to address them in Q1 2025. Higher-than-expected care activity and changes in patient profiles have led to a revision in EPS guidance. Despite these headwinds, management emphasized its confidence in addressing these issues and improving performance into 2026. Investors should monitor the execution of these strategies and the impact of ongoing changes in Medicare Advantage and Optum Health operations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DoE preparing $10B clean energy funding cuts which could hurt Occidental, Exxon projects - WSJ
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Energy is preparing dramatic cuts that could halt nearly $10B in federal funding for clean energy projects including hydrogen, carbon capture, long-duration energy storage and other technologies, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

Among government contracts at risk of losing DoE funding are those with Exxon Mobil (XOM) to use hydrogen for fuel at a large ethylene plant, Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) for carbon capture in south Texas, and NextEra Energy (NEE) for long duration storage, according to the report.

The DoE also is said to be considering withdrawing funding from four regional hydrogen hubs, largely in Democratic-leaning regions, while continuing to help fund three hydrogen hubs in largely Republican areas, and more than 250 projects tied to electric vehicle charging, wave energy, battery recycling, solar, wind and other technologies also are reportedly at risk.

The cuts are part of a broad campaign by President Trump's Department of Government Efficiency to shrink the scale of the government and its spending, which already has resulted in thousands of job cuts across the federal workforce.

Most of the DoE's grants and loans have been in limbo since February, when Trump signed an executive order pausing the release of federal funds appropriated under the Infrastructure Improvement and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act that were approved during the Biden administration.

data.title: 17 of 19 financial stocks post earnings wins as banks win big -Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
In this week's earnings recap, 19 companies belonging to the S&P 500 financial sector (NYSEARCA:XLF) reported their earnings for the period ended March.

In the first quarter, XLF had advanced 3.2%, as compared to SPY's fall of 4.3%. QTD, XLF has fallen 6.5%, as compared to SPY's 6.3% fall.

Earnings recap

Out of the 19 financial companies belonging to the S&P 500 index that reported results this week, 17 of them reported an earnings beat, while two missed.

On the revenue side, 12 companies exceeded revenue consensus, while seven companies trailed estimates.

Below are the latest reports from five companies that reported their results:

Bank of America (BAC) turned in bottom-line and top-line beats, helped by sales and trading revenue growth at its Global Markets division.

Citigroup (C) reaffirmed 2025 guidance, and its Q1 earnings and revenue beat consensus estimates, with strong performance in its markets and investment banking businesses.

Goldman Sachs' (GS) Q1 earnings and revenue significantly outperformed Wall Street consensus estimates, reflecting robust increases in the bank's fixed income, currency, and commodities revenue as well as equity revenue.

Blackstone's (BX) Q1 earnings beat the Wall Street consensus as the private equity firm pulled in its highest levels of inflows in almost three years amid a volatile market.

American Express (AXP) Q1 earnings and revenue on Thursday beat the average analyst estimate, as its net interest income and card fee revenue rose as its provision for credit losses declined.

At the industry level, 13 banks, two capital markets, one consumer finance company, and three insurance companies reported earnings this week.

"XLF is showing relative strength, outperforming broader indices and topping in early March," Seeking Alpha analyst MacroGirl said in early March.

QTD, 24 out of 43 companies that have reported earnings so far are a part of the financial sector.

Despite risks, XLF's positive backdrop and relative strength indicate it could continue to perform well if the economy avoids recession, MacroGirl added.

Financial companies reporting next week include Capital One Financial (COF), Fiserv (FI), Chubb (CB), Discover Financial Services (DFS), and Nasdaq (NDAQ) among others.

data.title: Judge rules that Google has illegally monopolized online ad space
data.description:
Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) has been dealt a blow after a judge overseeing a Department of Justice antitrust case ruled that the tech giant has held monopoly in online advertising technology in at least two markets.

U.S. District Judge Leonie Brinkema in Alexandria, Virginia, has found that Google broke the law by dominating ad spaces all over the internet by having an illegal stronghold in markets for publisher ad servers and the market for ad exchanges.

"With the benefit of a three-week bench trial and extensive post-trial filings, the Court finds that Plaintiffs have failed to prove that there is a relevant market for open-web display advertiser ad networks but have proven that Google violated Section 2 of the Sherman Act by willfully acquiring and maintaining monopoly power in the open-web display publisher ad server market and the open-web display ad exchange market and has unlawfully tied its publisher and server (DFP) and ad exchange ((AdX)) in violation of Sections 1 and 2 of the Sherman Act," according to the court filing.

"Plaintiffs have proven that Google has willfully engaged in a series of anticompetitive acts to acquire and maintain monopoly power in the publisher ad server and ad exchange markets for open-web display advertising. For over a decade, Google has tied its publisher ad server and ad exchange together through contractual policies and technological integration, which enabled the company to establish and protect its monopoly power in these two markets," Judge Leonie Brinkema wrote.

The loss in the ad tech case adds to Google's woes as it braces for another trial next week in Washington related to its online search business, where the DOJ is trying to convince judges to make the company sell its Chrome browser and take other necessary action to curb anticompetitive practices.

data.title: Tariff tough: Dollar Tree is outperforming Amazon, Walmart and Costco over the last six weeks
data.description:
Dollar Tree (NASDAQ:DLTR) gained 6.4% in midday trading on Thursday and is now up more than 25% over the last six weeks as investors continue to drift toward the consumer staples stock amid macroeconomic and tariff uncertainty.

Dollar Tree (NASDAQ:DLTR) has generated an 18% gain over the last six weeks to easily top the returns of broad retail (XRT), Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and Costco (COST) over the same period.

 

 

Notably, Dollar Tree (LDTR) has been aggressive in preparing for the tariff scenario by negotiating supplier cost concessions, adjusting sourcing, changing product specifications, and dropping non-economical items.

Last week, Citi highlighted that high tariff rates give Dollar Tree (DLTR) some cover to boost prices in key product categories. "While Dollar Tree was previously trying to manage China tariffs within its current pricing architecture, this higher tariff regime gives them further cover to expand price points from $1.25 to $1.50 - $1.75," highlighted analyst Paul Lejuez.

Seeking Alpha analyst Redfox Capital Ideas said the multi-price-point strategy means consumers are willing to purchase more products at the $3 and $5 price point level, while still considering the pricing a good deal or value purchase.

Other positive factors for Dollar Tree (DLTR) over the last few weeks are insider buying from CFO Stewart Glendinning and a significantly reduced level of overlap for the company with Chinese e-commerce player Temu. Looking ahead, Dollar Tree (DLTR) is seen potentially benefiting from store improvements and new stores entering the comparable base, including 99 Cents Only stores. 

As for near term catalysts, Deutsche Bank thinks the Dollar Tree (DLTR) could add more details about the impact of the Family Dollar sale during the company's upcoming earnings conference call.

Shares of Dollar Tree (DLTR) still trade well below the 52-week high mark of $124.81, despite the recent rally. Rival Dollar General (DG) is up 18% over the last six weeks and is in positive territory for the year.

data.title: Ally Financial stock slips after lower loss provision helps drive Q1 earnings beat
data.description:
Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) slid 2.5% in Thursday midday trading after the company's Q1 earnings topped the Wall Street consensus, as it set aside less money than expected for bad debt.

The auto finance company reaffirmed its full-year 2025 guidance for net interest margin (ex-original issue discount) of 3.40%-3.50%, flat adjusted other revenue, retail auto net charge-off rate of 2.00%-2.25%, and consolidated net charge-off rate of 1.35%-1.50%.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.58, vs. the average analyst estimate of $0.42, decreased from $0.78 in Q4 2024 and increased from $0.41 in Q1 2024.

Q1 adjusted total net revenue of $2.07B, beating the $1.97B consensus estimate, compared with $2.09B in the previous quarter and $2.00B a year ago.

Q1 adjusted provision for credit losses fell to $191M, far lower than the Visible Alpha consensus of $540.7M, from $557M in Q4 and $507M in last year's Q1.

The lower loan loss provision and lower taxes helped offset higher expenses and lower net interest income, said Evercore ISI analyst John Pancari.

Net financing revenue (excluding core OID) fell to $1.49B from $1.52B in the prior quarter and edged up from $1.48B a year ago.

Net interest margin, excluding core OID, was 3.31% in Q1 2025, up from 3.30% in Q4 and 3.16% in Q1 2024.

Core return on tangible common equity of 8.3% vs. 11.3% in Q4 and 5.9% in last year's Q1.

Noninterest expense of $1.63B climbed from $1.36B in the previous quarter and $1.31B a year ago.

By segment:

  • Auto Finance pretax income of $375M vs. $397M in the prior quarter and $480M a year ago.
  • Insurance pretax income of $2M vs. $36M in Q4 and $70M in last year's Q1.
  • Corporate Finance pretax income of $76M vs. $120M in the prior quarter and $100M a year ago.

Earlier, Ally Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $0.58 beats by $0.16, revenue of $1.54B misses by $430M

data.title: Simmons First National Corporation targets mid-teens PPNR growth for 2025 amid proactive credit measures
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simmons First National Corporation (SFNC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO George Makris emphasized the company's continued commitment to soundness, profitability, and growth. Management highlighted proactive measures taken to address credit challenges, including boosting specific reserves for two non-performing loans, which resulted in an additional $15.6 million provision expense for the quarter.
  • President Jay Brogdon noted that commercial loan pipelines grew 43% linked quarter, reaching their highest level since Q2 2022. Customer deposits increased by $183 million during the quarter, reflecting a 4% linked quarter annualized growth. The company achieved a net interest margin of 2.95%, an increase of 8 basis points quarter-over-quarter.
  • CFO Daniel Hobbs stated, "We are confident in our level of reserves based on today's circumstances." He reinforced the commitment to expense management and noted that adjusted non-interest expense, excluding a fraud event, showed slight improvement compared to Q4 2024.
  • Management announced that the company's original 2025 outlook, including mid-teens year-over-year growth in pre-provision net revenue (PPNR) and positive operating leverage, remains intact.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its 2025 guidance of achieving over 3% net interest margin earlier than anticipated, supported by favorable asset repricing and positive trends in customer deposits.
  • The company expects to resolve the two highlighted credit issues within the year, with a focus on achieving optimal outcomes for recovery.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue increased by $1.1 million compared to the prior quarter, marking the fourth consecutive quarter of top-line adjusted revenue growth.
  • Non-interest income grew 6% quarter-over-quarter, driven by swap fee income and diversified growth from fee-based businesses.
  • Adjusted non-interest expenses increased by $4.3 million due to a fraud event involving a borrower’s affiliated entities. Excluding this, expenses were slightly lower than Q4 2024 levels.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about the timeline for resolving the two problematic credits. President Brogdon responded that while they aim for resolution by the end of the year, they will prioritize achieving the best possible outcomes.
  • Woody Lay, KBW: Inquired about the collateral for the fast-food operator loan. Brogdon explained the company has collateral in the form of real estate, stores, and equipment, but noted concerns over the guarantor's global cash flow.
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about the specific reserve process for the two non-performing loans. Brogdon emphasized a conservative approach, stating the reserves were set to minimize incremental loss risk.
  • Ahmad Hassan, D.A. Davidson: Questioned the lack of share repurchases during the quarter. Brogdon indicated that capital preservation remains a priority, with organic growth and dividend commitments taking precedence over buybacks.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but raised concerns about credit issues and the competitive deposit environment. Questions reflected skepticism about potential risks in the credit portfolio and the broader macroeconomic backdrop.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing proactive credit management and a strong outlook for 2025. During the Q&A, responses were measured and focused on providing clarity around credit resolutions and profitability strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter highlighted a stronger commercial loan pipeline and notable growth in customer deposits, compared to a more cautious tone in Q4 2024.
  • Management’s tone showed greater confidence in achieving guidance targets, while analysts remained focused on credit risks and deposit competition.
  • Adjusted non-interest expense management showed improvement despite the fraud incident, contrasting with a more stable expense outlook in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted two specific credit challenges involving a St. Louis hotel and a fast-food operator, both moved to non-performing status. Specific reserves were increased to approximately 60% for these loans.
  • Analysts noted the competitive deposit landscape as a continuing challenge and questioned the potential impact of macroeconomic uncertainty on credit quality.
  • Management emphasized ongoing efforts to mitigate these risks, including proactive credit monitoring and targeted growth in core deposits.

Final Takeaway

Simmons First National Corporation demonstrated resilience in Q1 2025, with strong loan pipeline growth and proactive credit management. Despite challenges, the company maintained its guidance for mid-teens PPNR growth and over 3% net interest margin, supported by disciplined expense management and strategic focus on soundness and profitability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
